items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: Maguire (2000) found enlarged ___ in London taxi drivers:
items.why: It's Hippocampi. “Amygdala” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 5-HTT and depression. “DRD4 and risk-taking” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Maguire publish her London taxi drivers study?
items.why: Exact answer: 2000 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
